title: Quick example
text:
macroDW <- readRDS(file = here("data/derived-data/macro_dw_raw.rds"))

# clean macros data.frame and aggregate abundances within sites by taxonID
macroDWList = macroDW %>% 
  dplyr::select(siteID, collectDate, sampleID, acceptedTaxonID,sizeClass, dw, no_m2) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(collectDate = as.Date(collectDate)) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(collectMonth = as.Date(
    paste0(
      lubridate::year(collectDate),
      "-",
      lubridate::month(collectDate),
      "-01"),
    format = "%Y-%m-%d"),
    collectYear = lubridate::year(collectDate)) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(no_m2 = sum(no_m2), .by = c(siteID, collectYear, acceptedTaxonID, sizeClass, dw)) %>% 
  named_group_split(siteID, acceptedTaxonID)

How many groups have only 1 size class?
2097


Here we walk through a procedure to estimate the maximum body size for each taxa using observations across years. For each taxa, we group all observations within a year to best capture the full potential sizes observed. Below is a histogram of all observed body sizes within this example.

## Which has the most observations?
max_r = map_int(macroDWList, ~nrow(.x)) %>% max();max_r

[1] 232

## practice workflow on a single, well-sampled taxa
x = Filter(function(x) nrow(x) == max_r, macroDWList) %>% purrr::flatten_df()

x %>% 
  ggplot()+
  geom_histogram(aes(x = sizeClass, y = ..density.., weight = no_m2))








We then go through a procedure to resample from the size distribution within each year. In this case, the sizes are resampled with replacement to a fixed length (e.g., 1000). We then add variability around the size bin by sampling from a uniform distribution \(\pm\) 0.5mm around the length class.

x_samp = x %>% 
  slice_sample(n = 100, by = collectYear, weight_by = no_m2, replace = TRUE)

x_list = x_samp %>% 
  named_group_split(collectYear) %>% 
  map(~.x %>%
        select(sizeClass) %>%
        mutate(sizeClass_est = midpoint_resample_vec(sizeClass)) %>% 
        select(sizeClass_est) %>% 
        unlist)

From here we fit a suite of models using the size vectors of body sizes. These models are outlined in Heather et al. 2026 “Beyond the single biggest fish: robust inference of species maximum length (\(L_{max}\)) from sample maxima”. That estimate maximum length using Bayesian inference with three different approaches. These have been collected into an R package fishmax.

fishmax::fit_max_model(x_list)

From these models we can look at the estimated maximum lengths with get_max()





model
max_fit.50%
max_lwr.10%
max_upr.90%




EVT (GEV)
40.97927
35.90196
48.73112


EVT (Gumbel)
42.74067
37.61129
49.67276


EFS
38.98511
35.38883
43.81292


EFSmm
52.45721
51.90712
53.00774





and lastly plot the estimates and their uncertainty with plot_max()

title: Data import
text:
This script outlines the process to download macroinvertebrate data from a working database of files released by NEON. The full process for updating this database can be found in the NEONSizeData repository. This process uses the neonstore package to manage data file download and storage for specific NEON data releases that form the underlying derived data products for size analysis. The database of up-to-date files is stored locally on a single computer and the updating of data files is automated. For downstream analysis, the s3 database will serve as the working data source.
We set the specifications of the s3 bucket. This bucket is publicly readable:

# set the s3 bucket specs  
bucket = "neonsizedata-392202703749-us-east-2-an"
prefix = "neonstore/parquet"

We can view the files available on the s3 bucket using the aws.s3 package to view the contents.

get_bucket(bucket = bucket,
           prefix = prefix,
           max = 3)

And we can connect to the s3 bucket to perform actions locally by connecting to the remote bucket:

s3 = arrow::s3_bucket(bucket = paste0(bucket,"/",prefix,"/"))
remote = neon_remote_db(bucket = s3)

and then reading in a file from the remote connection.

neon_remote(
  table = "inv_taxonomyProcessed-basic",
  product = "DP1.20120.001",
  db = remote
  )  %>%
  dplyr::collect() %>% 
  dplyr::glimpse()

title: NEON Invert Max Size
text:
The goal of NEON-invert-max-size is to build a workflow to estimate the maximum body size of macroinvertebrate taxa from stream sites in the National Ecological Observatory Network (NEON).
To do so, we use multiple methods:
1) Extreme Value Theory (EVT)
2) Exact Finite Sampling (EFS)
3) General mass-mortality scaling from a McKendrick-von Foerster transport model
These data are used to explore size-temperature scaling relationships across taxonomic groups and test predictions from macroecological theory on the drivers of body size variation (i.e. Bergmann’s Rule).

title: full-analysis
text:
Introduction

macroDWList <- readRDS(file = here('data/derived-data/macroDWList.rds')) 

# How many sampling years does each taxa-site combination have?
site_taxa_nYear_tab = macroDWList %>% 
  # keep only taxa with >= 3 years of observations
  map(~.x %>% 
        dplyr::select(collectYear) %>% 
        distinct %>% 
        unlist %>% 
        length()) %>% 
  bind_rows %>% 
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols= everything(), names_to = 'site-taxa', values_to = 'nYear') 

site_taxa_nYear_tab %>% 
  knitr::kable(., format = 'html', table.attr = "class='dtable'", col.names = c('Site-Taxa', 'nYear'), escape = FALSE)
